channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.mmorpg.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.mmorpg.com

item.title: World of Tanks: Heat's Closed Beta Test Is Live Now Through April 20th On PC And Consoles
item.content:encoded:
Xbox Series S|X and PlayStation 5 consoles, that is

Wargaming's next iteration of its tank-based combat, World of Tanks: HEAT, launched its closed beta test today and will run through April 20th. The event brings four game modes across eight maps, as well as access to its agents and tank roster to really hone in your playstyle ahead of launch.

During a presentation to the press earlier this week, Wargaming talked a bit about what you can expect in the beta this weekend. For those who haven't heard much about HEAT, it's Wargaming's take on a Tank-powered hero shooter, where players take the role of specialized agents in an alternate post-World War 2 history. While longtime World of Tanks fans might be more familiar with its slower-paced, deliberate gameplay, World of Tanks: HEAT is designed to be fast-paced and hard-hitting from the jump.  

Each agent has its own set of skills and abilities, as well as access to two tanks to even further augment your kit with modules to power up your vehicle. Agents can be broken down into three types: Assault, Defender, and Marksman. Assault is your fast-moving, fast-hitting Agent that specializes in offense, flanking, and repositioning, while your Defender agent, a literal tank class, which can absorb damage and push the objective. Marksman are your snipers, but also can help to lock down parts of a map to funnel enemy tanks into kill zones for your assault and defenders as well. All three appear to work synergistically with the right team makeup, and it'll be interesting to jump in myself this weekend and try my hand at all three.

Its four PvP modes are similar to what you might find in other competitive hero shooters: Hardpoint, which is a capture and defend mode of rotating bases; Control, which sees players fight to control a single zone; Kill Confirmed, which is a deathmatch-style mode that also requires you to not only kill the enemy tank, but claim their dog tag to "kill confirm;" and finally Conquest, the largest mode in the game featuring 10v10 teams battling it out over a dynamic warzone. 

The team tells me these maps are around 1 km x 1 km, making them rather large, which I wondered could present a challenge for the smaller 5v5 game modes. However, Wargaming confirmed that for those smaller game modes, the playable area is shrunk to accommodate, and it's not always the same playable area each time, making for dynamic fights and the need to learn the whole map and not just a repeatable chunk of it. 

I'm definitely interested in checking out the hero synergy, especially as we start to see builds take off over the course of the beta. While World of Tanks: Heat will only launch with three of its agents playable out of the gate (you'll need to unlock the others through gameplay), the beta will have the full roster available to test. While your agent will determine your passive and hero skills, including your ultimate, such as the Defender Chopper's Creeping Barrage,  or Assault agent Kent's passive that gives you a faster reload for each successive hit on an enemy, your choice of tank will also play a role in your Agent's total kit. As Wargaming put it on our call this week, World of Tanks: HEAT uses what it calls internally "human-machine synergy." 

While we don't have a release window just yet, World of Tanks: HEAT is confirmed to be releasing across a wide gamut of devices, from PC, with handheld PC support out of the gate, as well as Xbox Series X|S and PlayStation 5 consoles. It will also launch on GeForce Now, Nvidia's cloud-streaming service, though it won't be playable there during this weekend's beta. 

HEAT runs on a new proprietary engine designed from the ground up to be scalable across platforms, and Wargaming is not describing its non-PC versions as "ports," rather, these are bespoke editions of the game built for that platform. I asked about the Nintendo Switch 2 and whether HEAT could be coming there since it is supported on mobile gaming machines like the Xbox ROG Ally X and the Steam Deck, but Wargaming told me that Nintendo's platform is "not part of our current strategy," and there are "no concrete plans to go to Switch."

However, HEAT will be completely cross-play/cross-progression friendly, so there's no need to juggle multiple accounts and unlocks, rather it'll be one master account anywhere you decide to play. 

You can check out more details on the World of Tanks: HEAT website, as well as sign up to play in the closed beta test this weekend.

Joseph Bradford

Joseph has been writing or podcasting about games in some form since about 2012. Having written for multiple major outlets such as IGN, Playboy, and more, Joseph started writing for MMORPG in 2015. When he's not writing or talking about games, you can typically find him hanging out with his 15-year old or playing Magic: The Gathering with his family. Also, don't get him started on why Balrogs *don't* have wings. You can find him on Twitter @LotrLore



https://www.mmorpg.com/previews/world-of-tanks-heats-closed-beta-test-is-live-now-through-april-20th-on-pc-and-consoles-2000137816
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Sky: Children of the Light Players Can Build Their Own Challenges And Partake In Festivities In New Season of Carnival
item.content:encoded:
In the new long-term Season in the social adventure MMO Sky: Children of the Light arriving this week, players get to not only help out the Wandering Carnival’s festivities, but also make carnival-like challenges of their own. 

In this “Season of Carnival, players are beckoned to help set up the Wandering Carnival—and of course, when that’s done, they can partake in its activities and reap the rewards as well. 

Five Seasonal Quests will be unlocked throughout the season, on April 17, May 1, May 15, May 29 and June 12. Each brings different challenges to take on, including an obstacle course, some ball shooting, and hanging up flyers for the grand opening of the Carnival. 

Sky players will also get to create and join Shared Space Challenges, which will be available to build and play beyond the season. 

The Seasonal Quests will unlock different components for the Shared Spaces, including some items that’ll help create limitations like time limits for the sake of challenges, as well as obstacles and goals like Bounce Pads and Challenge Tokens. By using a Challenge Flag, players can join these specific player-built challenges. 

Welcome to the Wandering Carnival! ??Friendly faces have returned to put on a show for all Sky Kids, but they need your help to set up! Unleash your creativity as you build and participate in exciting new shared Space Space Challenges ?Are you ready? The carnival awaits ?? pic.twitter.com/4q5srU2qpp

— Sky: Children of the Light (@thatskygame) April 15, 2026

At the end of the season, at an unspecified date, players will be able to “enjoy the carnival alongside lots of other Sky Kids,” likely meaning there’ll likely be one last grand area to enjoy as many Seasons and events have. As usual, there’ll be an array of rewards to both earn and purchase in the shop. 

This new Season of Sky launches this Friday, April 17 and runs until July 2. Already, there’s the Earth Day event going on until April 20, and there’s been a major patch this week that adds some new social capacities and brings some fixes.



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/sky-children-of-the-light-players-can-build-their-own-challenges-and-partake-in-festivities-in-new-season-of-carnival-2000137815
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Titan Quest II's Summons Update is Live, With Reworked Epics, and Mastery Updates
item.content:encoded:
Summons are finally here in Titan Quest II. The fan-favorite feature from the original game has made it into the sequel in a big Early Access update.

Each Mastery gets a summon in today’s patch, which will eventually lead to the team making a full summoner playstyle in the game. According to their plans, this will be possible when the Spirit Mastery is released later this year. Today’s patch adds summons and options on how you might choose to play with them.

If you want a more passive playstyle and let your summons go out there and do the bulk of the fighting for you while you stay and react to  what's going on and issue commands, you can do that. if you want to get right in there and use your summons to fight alongside you, that’s also possible. The options depend on the summon, as sending out a War Banner for War Mastery will play differently than sending out the Core Dweller to pummel your enemies. 

The War Banner, Warfare Mastery’s summon, just stands there, healing and buffing allies that come within its aura, and nerfing enemies. The Core Dweller is a tanky rock golem summon that can do what you’d expect from a golem: slam enemies, casting fissures in your place, and can even be more powerful via channeling. Today’s update also adds the Wisp for Storm Mastery and the Shadow Clone for Rogue Mastery.

Talismans also arrive, a new off-hand choice you can use instead of a shield. These offer a guaranteed increase to energy, plus some buffs and can help a number of different builds,  especially useful for casters but non-casters can make use of them.

This is a pretty big update, and the patch notes go into all of the extensive info you’ll ever need. One of the other big parts of this update is a rework of Epics. Grimlore  has done a pass and buffed almost every one of them. Not only does the team run down on every single change made to Epics, affixes, and item stats, we get info on UI changes, fixes, and more.



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/titan-quest-iis-summons-update-is-live-with-reworked-epics-and-mastery-updates-2000137812
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
